summary: I was born in Hiroshima Prefecture as the first son of a poor farmer. Life there was hard because we owned less than an acre of hilly land, mostly growing rice and wheat. My grandmother raised me mostly, and I remember walking to Hiroshima city when I was a child to visit a neighbor wounded in the Russo-Japanese War. Even in the early 1900s, only a few houses on the main street had electricity.

My father went to Hawaii before I was born, around 1895 or earlier, as an immigrant to grow sugar beets under a three-year contract. Many young Japanese men lived in camps there, but conditions were rough and disorderly. After his contract ended, he returned to Japan, married my mother, and I was born. Later, my uncle also went to Hawaii as a contract immigrant before coming to America.

After my father returned to Japan and married, he decided to go to Canada when I was still very young, leaving my mother and me behind. He went to Canada around the time I was an infant, probably before 1904. From Canada, he eventually came to America. His move was partly because farming in Japan was tough due to taxes and poor conditions during and after the Sino-Japanese War.

I came to America in December 1910 after finishing six years of compulsory education in Japan. My father had already been in America for some years by then. I traveled alone by ship, arriving first in Seattle because the immigration exam in San Francisco was stricter. Life was hard for Japanese immigrants like my family, who faced rejection and language barriers. My uncle and mother also came to America later, and I lived with relatives before joining them.

The Sino-Japanese War from 1894 to 1895 made life very hard for poor Japanese farmers like my family. The government had to raise taxes to fund the war, which burdened common people. My father left home around that time, possibly because of these economic difficulties. While the rich landowners did well, we struggled to make ends meet during and after the war.

When I was about seven or eight years old, I visited a neighbor who was wounded and hospitalized in Hiroshima during the Russo-Japanese War. That was a memorable event because I got separated from my grandmother on the way home and cried. The war was a significant backdrop to my childhood in Hiroshima Prefecture.

Hiroshima city was about five miles from my village, and I remember walking there with my grandmother to visit a wounded neighbor during the Russo-Japanese War. At that time, Hiroshima was still developing; only a few houses on the main street had electricity. It was a place I passed through often as a child, and it represented the nearest urban center to my rural home in Hiroshima Prefecture.

When I left Japan to come to America in 1910, I departed from Kobe port. There was no pier at that time, so we had to take a rowboat out to the ship, which was about 6,000 tons. It was my first experience traveling overseas, and I didn't know much about America then because letters from there were written in classic Japanese, which I couldn't read.
The transcript does not mention Yokkaichi, so I have no memories or experiences related to that place.
The transcript does not include any references to Yokohama, so I cannot share any stories or experiences about it.

After arriving in America, I was told to go to Seattle because the immigration physical exam in San Francisco was stricter. The journey to Seattle took about two weeks by ship. I remember the ship's air smelled bad compared to the fresh air I was used to, and the food was different from what I had in Japan.

San Francisco was the main immigration port, but I was advised to avoid the physical examination there because it was stricter, especially for eye diseases like trachoma, which I had. Instead, I went to Seattle for immigration processing. I remember the difficulty of passing health exams in America at that time.
